title: The Education-Industrial Complex
contents:
I’ve just got to say it… when have you EVER purchased a ‘solution’ that made your education delivery system less complicated and more effective?

Talk about making things more complicated!! It’s another case of the cure being as bad as the disease. The “education-government complex” isn’t inevitable - it’s a created market that benefits everyone except those it should serve: teachers and students.
The Perfect Money-Making Machine
Here’s something important that many people miss: education has become a perfect money-making machine for companies and consultants that sell “solutions” to problems created by government mandates (funded or unfunded, it is all unnecessary work).
This cycle works like this:
Lawmakers create extremely detailed mandates (like the TEKS in Texas)
These mandates are too complex for teachers or administrators to track manually
Companies create expensive “solutions” to help schools keep track of it all
Schools must buy these solutions to meet state requirements
More mandates create more markets for more products
Taxpayers foot the bill while companies profit
It’s a perfect system - if you’re selling the solutions, not if you’re teaching or learning.
The Problem of Manufactured Complexity
I once asked a Superintendent why very experienced teachers need to continuously create detailed lesson plans. Came the one-word answer: “TEKS” (these are the state-mandated educational standards for public schools in Texas, outlining what students should know and be able to do in various subjects at each grade level, according to the Texas Education Agency).
Teaching today carries a load of unnecessary work. Let’s break this down:
An experienced math teacher knows how to teach algebra effectively
But now they must document exactly how each lesson covers each TEK
TEKS are constantly changing making text books and lesson plans outdated almost immediately
This creates hours of paperwork that doesn’t improve teaching
Enter the software and textbook companies with “alignment tools” that promise to save time and guarantee compliance with the TEKS
Schools buy these expensive programs and textbooks because they have no choice if they hope to stay ‘aligned’ with the TEKS
Teachers then spend hours feeding data into these systems, learning how to feed the data, and learning how to access and interpret the reports.
The state creates the complexity and the marketplace responds. Suddenly, there is money to be made from the ‘good intentions’ of the education authorities. For all intents and purposes, the state is in the software and book business.
The Education-Industrial Complex
This pattern resembles what President Eisenhower called the “military-industrial complex” - where government spending creates industries that then lobby for more spending. In education, we see:



Each player in this system except teachers and students benefits from more complexity, not less.
How This Hurts Teaching and Learning
This system has real costs beyond just money:
Teacher burnout: Experienced teachers quit when paperwork replaces teaching
Lost teaching time: Hours spent on compliance mean less time with students
Resource drain: Money spent on software could fund smaller classes or better materials
Focus shift: The goal becomes meeting mandates, not helping students learn
Innovation barriers: Creative teaching gets squeezed out by standardization
Teacher shortages: Teachers got into the business to teach, not feed computers
The Seductive Promise of Easy Solutions
The irony is these products are marketed with seductive promises:
“Save time on lesson planning!”
“Track student progress automatically!”
“Ensure compliance with all state standards!”
“Individualize learning with our algorithm!”
But the reality is different:
Teachers still spend hours inputting data into the systems
The “time-saving” tools create new forms of non-classroom work
Much of the data collected isn’t actually useful for teaching
The promised individualization rarely delivers meaningful results
Breaking the Cycle
How might we break this destructive cycle? Here are some starting points:
Simplify standards: Push for fewer, clearer standards that experienced teachers could reasonably follow without special tools
Question new purchases: When vendors promise solutions, ask “Would this problem exist without the mandate?”
Calculate true costs: Add up not just the software price but teacher time spent feeding these systems
Trust teachers more: Give experienced educators more professional judgment in how they meet broad learning goals
Look for free alternatives: Many open-source and free tools can replace expensive proprietary systems
Talking Points for Board Members and Administrators
When faced with the next “essential” education software or book purchase, try these questions:
“Would we need this if the state requirements were simpler?”
“How much teacher time will this system require to maintain?”
“Could we meet our goals with existing tools or simpler approaches?”
“Is this solving a real educational problem or a compliance problem?”
“What’s the evidence this improves actual student learning, not just compliance?”
Imagine, if you will…
lawmakers and bureaucrats fearful enough to change the astonishing COSTS of this phenomenon - for districts and people.
your district internally ‘pushing back’ against unnecessary complexity by strengthening resources where they actually improve teaching and learning.
your communities pushing back hard in the ballot boxes
Does this make sense? Feel free to comment below.

title: Self-Inflicted Wounds: How School Districts Hurt Themselves
contents:
“No man’s life, liberty or property are safe while the Legislature is in session.” ~ Gideon John Tucker


Corrolary: God save us from PhD candidates with access to statistical software.

The Road to Education Hell is Paved with Good Intentions (and PhD candidate theses)
Public education is staggering under the weight of good intentions - from all sides. Legislatures pile on mandates and so-called accountability requirements — all while funding dwindles. Politicians virtue-signal all their great improvements to education on one hand, while guaranteeing failure with the other. I guess it’s good work if you can get it.
Why do legislatures everywhere inflict enormous, destructive pressures on school districts? As usual, the culprit is good intentions. Unfortunately, good intentions don’t matter much when their practical effects destroy districts and their students.
Every school board knows that many of these outside pressures are:
Unnecessary (you cannot get desired results by edict - TEA how’s that working for you so far?)
Destructive (e.g., pitting demographic groups against each other)
Imposed (at this point, ‘local control’ is a sad joke)
Unfunded (this is an immoral practice and must be resisted with vigor)
Even if you can clearly identify these problems for your district, good luck persuading a legislator that their “good intentions” are toxic! It takes courage and character to admit you had it wrong all along.
So, we leave that battle to ‘Advocacy’ (aka, hat-in-hand-groveling)
Looking in the Mirror
Here’s an interesting truth: boards and administrators can also increase the burdens on their own districts. These are self-inflicted wounds—and can be avoided. Here are some ‘time wasters’ you can avoid starting now:
#1 - Don’t Waste Time with So-called ‘Best Practices’
Boards and Districts are often seduced by so-called “best practices”, a business cliche pedaled within the education industry. It’s another corporate buzz word with no clear meaning. Like all buzz words, they sound good. Really, who could be against best practice?
In actual practice though they always add unnecessary burdens on staff and volunteers. They can even pit Trustees against each other in subtle (and not so subtle) ways. Ask yourself - are there more flowery words and virtue signalling before or after ‘best practices’ are put into place?
Reserve best practices for purely clerical or simple compliance mechanics. Avoid them for substantive policies. There have been uncounted successful organizations long before ‘best practices’ became a ‘thing.’
#2 - Don’t Waste Time with ‘Good Governance’ (or eXceptional governance either)
These are the latest forms of Administrative Theater. They even made up a new name: Boardsmanship. I would spell it differently. This snake oil is just a “Bureaucratic Pacifier” - activities that calm anxieties without addressing real issues.
In practice it is an aggressive attempt to convince boards that if they just perform a checklist of activities, state them aloud at board meetings, and weave them into their formal practices standardized test scores will improve. There is absolutely no evidence that shows that one leads to the other.
If fact, rationally it is far more likely that the causation goes the opposite way. The data equally supports the theory that relatives and friends of those students who score well in standardized tests are more likely to become board trustees. Successful students often have involved parents who become trustees. What is most surprising is not that these claims are virtually unproven, but that no convincing evidence is provided, nor asked for.
#3 - Don’t Waste Time Copying Others
It seems easy to copy others - no need to reinvent the wheel, right? Consider first that these ‘best practices’ evolved in a different place, with different people, with different priorities, with different methods, with different communities, and a theory of practice. So, where did the first people with ‘best practices’ come to have best practices? They didn’t buy them from someone else.
Presumably, through self-examination, testing, improving methods and processes, measuring results, and refining THEIR company practice. They know from earned experience that for them, these are best practices. But since you have a completely different system of people, methods, processes, etc. it is highly unlikely that those same practices are better than those you can prepare.
The REAL solution is to take the time to develop the best practices for YOUR people, in YOUR district, with YOUR resources.
Avoid copying others; it is dangerous and wasteful. There’s little evidence it will work well in your district. Instead, find out for yourself what works in your district; with YOUR people, parents, students, community, buildings and equipment. Districts will be much better off when they learn that EVERY organization, including their own, is ‘one-of-a-kind’. What you do with it is more in your control than you think.
#4 - Don’t Waste Time on so-called ‘evidenced-based’ practices
School boards are extremely vulnerable to the ‘reassurance’ that policies or procedures are ‘evidenced based’, giving the sales pitch a scientific gloss. I’m not sure why this phrase is so effective in reassuring the education community. When folks go out of their way to reassure you about something they should be doing as a matter of course, don’t fall for it.
Apparently, there are very few folks in the education space with either the time, inclination, training or experience to evaluate such claims.
I see no challenges to such obviously manipulative phrases except in the technical journals. When is the last time you had board training that taught evaluation techniques for such claims? I have now read numerous education research articles, and even several PhD theses. I see very little scholarship in them. The social sciences continue to struggle with the near impossibility of establishing cause-and-effect with human populations. Disappointingly, many claim otherwise.
All who consider adopting such nostrums should first consider what the phrase even means, and why it was invented. Keep in mind that there was evidence that the earth was flat, that the earth was the center of the solar system, that disease was spread by ‘bad air’. As it turns out, those ‘evidence-based’ theories turned out to be completely wrong.
Most claims of ‘evidence based’ practices strongly imply (and some boldly state) that if you follow their recommendations you will get the benefits promised. At its best, evidence is strong and rigorous. At its worst, it is weak and manipulative. Every racist, eugenicist, Nazi and bigot claims they have ‘evidence’ to support their prejudices, but it is hardly worth the name evidence.
These claims may not stand up to scientific scrutiny, but they certainly can make careers and bring consulting work to those who make such claims.
Imagine, if you will…
your district with no self-inflicted wounds
Does this make sense? Feel free to comment below.

title: 'The Best Interests of the Child'
contents:
This cliche can completely thwart an improvement effort. It is another form of lying as it presumes we know far more than we can know. It is a troubling phrase that has moved from courts into schools. As usual, it enters the education system via an industry seen as more rigorous. This simple-sounding phrase creates big problems when school boards use it to guide decisions.
Where It Came From and How It’s Misused
This legal standard started in family law for custody decisions when children were at risk. In that narrow setting, with specific children and specific problems, courts could at least try to weigh real factors. Even there, many have criticized it for being too subjective.
In education, the phrase is even more problematic when:
Applied to thousands of different children at once
Not tied to specific, measurable results
Used by people who don’t know particular children well
Used to justify almost any position
Boards - This Shuts Down Debate
“In the best interests of the child” works mainly as a debate tactic that:
Stops discussion: Who would argue against children’s interests?
Claims moral high ground: Makes the speaker sound like a child advocate
Avoids being specific: Doesn’t define what those interests actually are
Can’t be tested: Creates a standard that can’t be proven wrong
The phrase works as a blank check. And, it stops conversation rather than starting it.
The Practical Problems
For school boards, the phrase creates several real problems:
1. No One Can Define It
What exactly is a child’s “best interest”? Does it mean:
Better test scores?
Emotional well-being?
Future earning potential?
Current happiness?
Social skills?
Matching family values?
Preparation for citizenship?
Without saying which interests matter most (and admitting they sometimes conflict), the phrase means nothing as a decision-making tool.
2. It Ignores Necessary Tradeoffs
Educational decisions always involve tradeoffs. Resources are limited, and focusing on one area means less focus on others:


Priority


Potential.Benefits


Potential.Costs


Academic rigor


Content mastery, college readiness


Stress, less time for other activities


Social-emotional support


Emotional skills, relationship skills


May mean less academic content coverage


Arts education


Creative expression, cultural appreciation


May mean fewer resources for core subjects


Athletics


Physical health, teamwork


Budget costs, injury risks


Vocational training


Practical skills, career preparation


May mean fewer resources for traditional academics


The “best interests” phrase implies we can maximize everything at once, which simply isn’t true.
3. It Assumes Everyone Agrees
The phrase assumes we all agree about what’s best for children, when in reality:
Families have different values and priorities
Communities have different needs and resources
Cultures have different expectations
Individual children have different strengths and challenges
What one parent views as essential, another may view as harmful or wasteful.
4. It Often Hides Adult Interests
Perhaps most troubling, the phrase often covers up adult interests:
Political agendas beyond education
Professional advancement opportunities
Avoiding difficult conversations
Protection from criticism or lawsuits
When these adult interests hide behind child-centered language, honest debate becomes impossible.
The Money Problem
From a budget perspective, “in the best interests of the child” is particularly dangerous because it:
Rejects budget limits as less important than children’s needs
Creates moral pressure to approve spending
Makes cost-benefit analysis seem anti-child
Expands school missions beyond core educational functions
School boards have a duty to be responsible with public money that can’t be abandoned for undefined “interests.”
A Better Way: Be Specific and Transparent
Instead of this vague, emotional phrase, boards should demand:
Specific goals: “This program aims to improve reading by X%”
Clear measurements: “Success will be measured by these 3 indicators…”
Honest tradeoffs: “Doing this means we’ll have to reduce that…”
Evidence: “Research suggests this works because…”
Resource needs: “This will cost X dollars and Y staff hours…”
Examples of Better Discussions
Instead of:
“We need this new social-emotional curriculum in the best interests of our children.”
Ask for:
“Is it the responsibility of the district to provide what children normally get elsewhere? Is the district the best choice for providing this? What specific results will this improve? How will we measure success? What tradeoffs are involved? What evidence shows it works? What resources will it take?”
When Someone Uses This Phrase
When someone says “the best interests of the child,” board members should respond with:
“Could you tell us which specific interests you’re talking about?”
“How would we measure whether we’re achieving those interests?”
“What evidence suggests this approach serves those interests better than others?”
“What tradeoffs should we consider in pursuing these particular interests?”
“How do you balance competing interests that might conflict?”
The phrase “in the best interests of the child” is a warning sign of fuzzy thinking or manipulation. While children’s needs must remain central to educational decisions, vague appeals to undefined “interests” block rather than help boards govern.
Imagine if you will…
How effective boards could be if they moved from Empty Words to Real Substance
By insisting on specificity, evidence, measurable results
By insisting on discussing tradeoffs honestly
Move toward real decision-making that truly serves students, families, and communities.
Does this make sense? Feel free to comment below.

title: Don't Squander Your Most Precious Resource
contents:
“Children are the world’s most valuable resource, and the best hope for the future” ~ attributed to John F. Kennedy

Soaring rhetoric for a politician, but serious trouble for school boards. This one can throw malassas into the gears in a hurry.
What Is the District’s ‘Most Valuable Resource’?
The overused cliche “our children are our most valuable resource” sounds noble but leads to nothing but trouble. First, children are NOT resources and they certainly do not ‘belong’ to school districts. There was a time when more children meant more hands to work the fields, but those days are long gone.
Words like these mean trouble for a district because they create a ‘blank check’ mentality where no amount of time or money is too much to spend in “the best interests of the child” (another cliche). Sometimes even the public gets this impression from board discussions.
The truth is, Time and Money are the only resources a Board actually has control over.
With their money, districts try to buy the best talent, wisdom, equipment and facilities they can afford. After that, the only resource left to them is Time. There will be more money. The time spent each year will never come again. No student, teacher, staff, administrator, superintendent, or board member will ever get that time back. The ‘whatever it takes’ mentality squanders the one nonrenewable resource available to boards.

Interesting Note: The politicians have generated a clamor for ‘Accountability’ and demand extensive disclosure of all financial matters. How about ‘Accountability’ for the wasted time imposed on a district that has to deliver an education that is worth something?
The state legislature needs an ‘Accountability Rating’ that reflects the amount of time their mandates consume that are not for direct instruction. I’d love to see their faces when that report came out!

This practical principle, used in place of the old cliche, offers a better framework. Let me explore why this shift matters and where it might lead us.
The Hidden Cost of “Whatever It Takes”
The “whatever it takes” approach to education sounds like a virtue but hides a damaging assumption: that the time available to the district is infinite. This simply isn’t true. No one pretends that Money is infinite. So, why do we pretend time is boundless?
How this can harms us:
Teachers, staff, administration, superintendents, and Trustees burn out from endless demands
Quality suffers as people stretch themselves thin
Important work gets the same priority as busywork
Real costs remain hidden and unmanaged
Quality of life declines for everyone
Better practice:
Treating time as your most precious resource would transform education in your district. The important questions would become:
Will this task, mandate, policy or strategy increase or reduce time spent in direct instruction?
Will it reduce or increase time spent on compliance or strictly administrative matters?
Will the time spent erode time for family and personal pursuits?
What programs will this new effort pull time from?
Central planning from Austin doesn’t help
Over-regulation by the state and federal education bureaucracies produces a difficult compression of time for districts. Since money is limited, districts cannot hire additional people to deal with new mandates. The only way districts can stay in compliance is to take more time from existing employees and volunteers.
Imagine Time-Conscious School Boards
If school boards truly valued time as their scarcest resource, meetings, planning and operations would change dramatically:
Boards would constantly find ways to do business responsibly with dramatically tighter agendas
Board members would be ultra-sensitive to staff and administrators’ time
Most super-detailed ‘transparency’ presentations could be reduced to an executive summary with supporting material available for public or further board review outside of meeting time.
‘Soapbox’ speeches would disappear
Virtue-signaling multiple ‘activity reports’ by board members would cease; a designated person can update the public on recent and future events of common interest.
Every proposal would include a time impact analysis, not just a dollar impact assessment
“Nice to have” initiatives would face additional scrutiny on their effects on other parts of the system
Value-added activities (direct instruction would be protected)
The shift from “Is this a ‘good’ thing to do?” to “Is this worth the cost in time?” would transform decision-making.
The Classroom Time Protection Act
Classroom time. If we truly valued our people’s time, we’d treat the teacher-student interaction like gold. We would:
Question every pull-out from classroom teaching
Fight against testing that doesn’t directly improve learning
Push back on documentation that doesn’t help students learn
Create buffers against administrative intrusions by the state
Measure the cost of compliance in teacher hours, not just dollars
Create grading methods that make rational use of instructor time
When a new state mandate arrives, the first question would be: “How many hours will this consume that could have been spent in direct instruction?”
Time Can Be an Employee (and volunteer) Benefit
There seems to be an automatic reaction that increasing teacher and staff financial compensation will solve most of our problems. Some argue low compensation is the primary reason for teacher shortages. Even though it is an extrinsic reward it should not be ignored. However, the history of public school instruction tells a story of high intrinsic motivation in education. People who want to make a lot of money do not become teachers in K-12 public education (they become college professors or politicians).
In an environment of shrinking money supply (thank you state legislature) we do have control of another way of rewarding employees and volunteers - with time.
In normal times, when salaries keep pace with inflation, having ‘three months off’ during the year is adequate time for family and recharging the batteries. When salaries do not keep pace with inflation educators have little choice but to increase their motivation for money. It is a sane response to a difficult situation. Time, however, is also valued by educators.
In a mature industry such as education, it is very difficult to think that things could be any way other than they way its ‘always been.’ Still, Boards with sufficient leadership can leverage time to great advantage to their districts.
Strategies for Creating More Time
The best way to create more time is to become more effective. It is a demonstrated fact that increasing quality will decrease costs (including time). Notice I didn’t say ‘efficient.’ You can be 100% efficient at doing the wrong things. I guess you’d call that doing the wrong things righter?
Becoming more effective is not easy. It takes time, training, leadership and commitment to understand how to examine and refine the systems of delivery, not the people delivering.
You cannot reap the benefits of being more effective if no time is available to do that work.
What Boards Could Do Tomorrow
If a board wanted to put a time-conscious approach into action, they could:
Audit time costs: Ask for an honest accounting of where teacher time goes now
Create a time impact statement: Require all new initiatives to have time cost estimates (even crude estimates are valuable)
Set time protection priorities: Identify core activities that deserve time protection, including board meeting time
Push back on unfunded time mandates: When the state requires new tasks, demand that existing tasks be eliminated or fully funded.
Make time gifts intentional: Consider “time back” as a deliberate benefit when raises aren’t possible
Guarding Time Like Money
Perhaps the most valuable shift would be normalizing the statement: “I don’t think this is worth the time drain on direct instruction.” Currently, this sounds almost heretical in education. But in fields where time is explicitly valued, it’s a normal part of prioritization.
The hardest but most important step is creating a culture where people can honestly assess whether an activity adds enough value to justify its time cost. This requires:
Strong board leadership that protects members who say no to some activities
Clear priorities that help people make time allocation decisions
Leaders who model time-conscious behavior themselves (particularly at board meetings)
Metrics that regularly measure how the process is working, not just performance results
Valuing time as our most precious resource is both practical wisdom and a potentially transformative lens for education. By shifting from “no price too high” to “given student academic needs, is this worth the time cost,” schools could refocus on what truly matters: learning.
The greatest value boards can provide their educational communities isn’t just more money (though that helps too) - it’s the recognition that everyone’s time is finite and precious. When we truly act on that as a value, we find that both student achievement and teacher satisfaction improve, not because we’re doing less, but because we’re doing less of what doesn’t matter.
Where does time figure in as a resource for your board?
Imagine if…
Boards had the courage to value Time
How would your board meetings change (maybe a lot shorter)?
Would it transform the board’s relationship with employees and volunteers?
Does this make sense? Feel free to comment below.

title: Want to Improve?  Begin with the Truth
contents:
Anything that garbles communication from one person to another, or one unit to another, or one department to another, or one teacher to another, or one campus to another, or one community group to another will make your system worse.
With a few simple word changes districts would immediately benefit from higher quality, lower costs and less fruitless effort. The results would also include much happier people; trustees, executive leadership, staff, teachers, parents, partners and students.
Use Simple, Direct Language (words matter more than you think)
Most board members know this: Simple words create better decisions and actions. Word choices determine how well we communicate our principles and practices. Efforts of school boards everywhere improve when simple, clear language is used.
Yet, too often boards adopt vision statements, mission statements, values statements, slogans and policies written by others who make their living teaching the latest buzzwords, cliches, and slogans. Some districts actually pay a lot of money to consultants to provide them (and the community) with dozens of obscure buzzwords and unrealistic slogans. They all make communication worse.
More often than not, those words are subtle lies. No one really believes that EVERY student will respond to district efforts equally. No one believes that EVERY student will have equal results. No one believes that districts can make EVERY student graduate with state-defined characteristics. No one believes that different groups of people will score the same under any conditions. For heaven’s sake, children in the same household can make wildly different scores after attending the same classes, often with the same teachers.
Yet, every day, everywhere, you can see and hear such statements in districts, from top to bottom. Before education became a pseudo-science no one spoke or wrote about education that way. They were down-to-earth citizens trying their best to preserve the district as a basic community resource for those who sought to build their own lives in their own ways. Now, our ‘betters’ in the legislature (guided by the education industry) are here to TELL us how we should prepare our kiddos for life.
Such words are fed to boards by the education industry under the faulty premise that boards can actually control the final results of education. They cannot. Maybe there is social pressure to follow such fantasies, but districts suffer the dysfunctions such statements create.
Board members must find their own words based on their own judgement of what the community wants - with their people, community and resources.
In other words - Think for Yourselves. The collective wisdom of boards for decades has produced all the geniuses, wealth, heroes, social fabric and opportunity that made this country the greatest of all time. Honestly, I think the education industry would heave a giant sigh of relief if the buzzwords, cliches, psycho-babel, newspeak, woke-isms, bastardized corporate jargon, and weasel words disappeared tomorrow. They could go back to doing what they set out to do - education.
Words that overstate, obscure, or have no common shared meaning
Using such words is also a subtle form of lying. Avoid it.
Here’s an example:
Typical of today - using obscure words that overstate the capabilities and promises of the education system:
“…to empower every student with the skills, confidence, and passion to thrive in their individual pursuits.”
Feels good to say it, doesn’t it?

This time with simple, direct words that state fairly the capabilities and intent of the education system and honors the role of the individuals involved:
“…to provide the opportunity for each student to develop the abilities, self-assurance, and experience they need to follow the ambition they hold for themselves.”

Everyone from the Trustee to the student can know what each word means and how it applies to them.
It clearly lays out the responsibilities of the parties: we will ‘provide the opportunity’ for each student, not ‘empower’ every student (which is impossible). The student and parents share the responsibility of developing these attributes in a child. We can only provide an opportunity; we cannot guarantee the result of ‘empowerment’ (whatever that means).
A Simple Word Test for Trustees
You may feel out of step with current education elites, but if you’re serious about making things better for your school district eliminate the buzzwords, cliches, psycho-babel, newspeak, woke-isms, bastardized corporate jargon, and weasel words from your materials, discussions and reports.
If you haven’t used the word during normal conversations in your home, or with friends, in the last month DON’T USE IT TO GOVERN YOUR DISTRICT!

Think about it: When’s the last time you used the words ‘empower’, ‘circle back’, ‘deep dive’, ‘robust’ or ‘outcomes’ around your kitchen table or watching football with your friends? And how about ‘boardsmanship’, or ‘actionable!’

A Cautionary Tale
Beware the Wordsmiths
Whenever consultants (or activists) roll out a new ‘solution’ they invent new words and phrases hoping you will perceive the ideas as new and important. Often they have technical meanings in other, more precise or business industries, but no common usage in education. For example, currently the ‘wordsmiths’ lump trustees, executive leadership, staff, teachers, parents, partners and students (who are actual flesh-and-blood human beings) into the sterile corporate jargon of ‘stakeholders’.
It’s certainly shorter than reciting each category every time, BUT should you be treating these people as all the same? If you believe that they are all alike, keep using ‘stakeholders’. If you believe they are distinct groups who require different considerations, ditch the word ‘stakeholders.’

Think about it: The urge to exaggerate reminds me of someone who thinks their name lacks importance, so they add the word “Captain” to it!
Ever notice how it is now no longer sufficient to be a diligent, steady, hard worker? You now must have ‘passion’. The ‘passion’ is held by the person insisting on using that word!

Imagine, if you will…
What message is sent to students when boards adopt slogans, goals, and statements that are idealistic instead of realistic?
Board meetings where trustees have confidence that the aims, principles and methods of the district are understandable and achievable. (e.g., you can know if you are providing an opportunity; you cannot know how the opportunity will unfold for each student)
Where the aims and methods of the board can be clearly communicated to Superintendents.
Where the aims and methods of the district can be clearly communicated to employees by Superintendents.
Where the aims and methods of the district can facilitate work between class levels, campuses, departments.
Does this make sense? Feel free to comment below.
